name: RIBBON RIVER - endless procedural Bezier ribbon winds through viewport, cards pin to it
ref: Codrops 'Building an Endless Procedural Snake with Three.js and WebGL' (February 2026, tympanus.net/codrops/2026/02/10); procedural-curve portfolio pattern cited in Codrops Creative Hub 2026 (tympanus.net/codrops/hub/)
brief: A single continuously winding organic Bezier ribbon is drawn to a Canvas 2D layer using a recursive subdivision curve built from random control points that are gently animated over time so the ribbon breathes slowly. Work cards are pinned to positions along the ribbon path computed via a 2D CatmullRom spline tangent, rotating to match the tangent angle. Scroll advances a path offset so the ribbon flows upward and new segments generate procedurally while old segments disappear below. Distinct from elastic-ribbon (one static CSS cord the visitor drags and snaps through; card nav, not a living river) and svgmap-journey (a static hand-drawn atlas map with waypoints; no procedural generation). The ribbon with several pinned cards is live at scroll=0. Canvas 2D only; no WebGL. Mobile: canvas scales to 50vw ribbon width. Guardrail: path point count capped at 200; old segments dropped once below viewport; reduced-motion freezes ribbon winding; any SVG pin marker fill/stroke must use hex not CSS var() in a presentation attribute. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: LIQUID PAGE - shader-dissolve multi-page transitions, each page a full distinct scene
ref: Codrops 'Building a Scroll-Revealed WebGL Gallery with GSAP, Three.js, Astro and Barba.js' (February 2026, tympanus.net/codrops/2026/02/02); multi-page WebGL transition portfolio pattern in Codrops Creative Hub 2026 (tympanus.net/codrops/hub/)
palette: #070708 (void) + scene-specific accent per page: page 1 amber #e8b84b, page 2 cyan #00d4ff, page 3 coral #ff6b6b; transitions are the palette shift
brief: A multi-page SPA (index + 3 work detail pages) where navigating between pages triggers a full-viewport WebGL shader transition: a noise-masked dissolve sweeping diagonally that reveals the next page's color accent. Each page is a distinct full-scene composition (index is a minimal type list; each detail is a full-bleed cinematic spread) so the transition IS the experience of moving between visual worlds. Distinct from view-transition-morph (single shared-element morphs between index card and detail; same color world; no WebGL) and fold-origami (within-page CSS 3D fold; single page). One detail page link is pre-hovered at scroll=0. WebGL: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays (ANGLE returns 0); use a LUT uniform for noise thresholds; preserveDrawingBuffer:false; canvas paints before render loop starts. Mobile: WebGL transition replaced by CSS clip-path diagonal wipe. Guardrail: one Three.js canvas persists across routes, only geometry/texture swaps; DPR capped at 1; routing via native History API pushState. Three.js via importmap; NO GSAP or external libs.

name: PAINT WORKLET - CSS Houdini paint worklets as live procedural borders and fills
ref: CSS Houdini Paint API (houdini.how community registry 2025-2026; developer.chrome.com/docs/css-ui/houdini); Houdini-based generative card borders seen across Awwwards creative-dev category 2026 (awwwards.com/inspiration_search/creative-developer/)
palette: #f4f0e8 (warm paper) + #111 (ink) + #d4a84b (amber) + #c0392b (vermilion); worklet output IS the decoration system
brief: Card borders, section dividers, and hover fills are driven by registered CSS Houdini paint worklets: one worklet draws a generative organic ink-bleed border that repaints on --seed and --progress custom properties, another fills the card hover state with a animated Voronoi noise pattern, all at zero DOM cost. The portfolio content is a clean typographic index; the procedural Houdini decoration is what makes it alive. Distinct from offscreen-canvas-substrate (a background noise field on a Worker thread behind floating cards) and tech-organic-mesh (a full-viewport GLSL mesh-gradient). The ink-bleed border worklet is already active on all cards at scroll=0. CSS.paintWorklet.addModule with a @property animation drives everything; no canvas element in the DOM; no WebGL. Mobile: identical (Paint API is mobile-Chrome supported). Guardrail: @supports (background: paint(x)) gate; fallback is a plain CSS border; reduced-motion freezes --progress at 0; worklet JS capped at 60 lines per worklet. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: SNAP SCORE - scroll-snap sections each with a distinct choreographed CSS entrance
ref: CSS scroll-snap choreography pattern cited in Codrops 'Scroll-Snap Is Here to Stay' (2026, tympanus.net/codrops/2026); Awwwards SOTD scroll-snap editorial layouts (awwwards.com/inspiration_search/scroll-snap/) 2026
palette: #0c0c0c (void) + per-section accent rotated through cobalt #1a3fc4, vermilion #c0392b, forest #1b5e35, amber #d4a84b + bone type #f0ece4
brief: A sequence of 6-8 full-viewport scroll-snap sections where EACH section has a distinct, authored CSS entrance choreography: section 1 rises from below with staggered word-clip-path, section 2 implodes from the four corners, section 3 types in monospace, section 4 irises open from center, section 5 unfolds like a triptych, section 6 drops cards from above in staggered delay. The snap itself is native CSS; the choreography is CSS @keyframes + animation-play-state toggled by an IntersectionObserver firing once per section. Distinct from scrollytelling-chapter (a long prose-column with sticky chapter rail; scroll choreography is per-work, not per-full-viewport-section) and fold-origami (a single within-scroll fold transition mechanic repeated). Each section's style is a genuinely different CSS motion primitive. The hero section entrance is mid-play at scroll=0. No canvas; no WebGL. Mobile: identical (snap fires natively). Guardrail: animation-play-state toggle fires once, no scroll listener in rAF; reduced-motion collapses every entrance to a plain opacity fade. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: LIVING GRID - container queries reshape card internals as gallery column count shifts
ref: CSS Container Queries (Baseline 2023, widely cited in Smashing Magazine 2026 component-driven layout articles smashingmagazine.com/2026); container-query responsive components seen in Codrops 'The Power of Container Queries in 2026' (tympanus.net/codrops/2026)
brief: A portfolio grid where a live resize handle lets the visitor drag the column count from 1 to 5. Each card uses CSS container queries (@container) to completely reshape its own internal layout at each breakpoint: at 1-col the card is a full editorial spread with large title and stacked metadata; at 2-col it becomes a side-by-side; at 3-col a compact thumbnail+caption; at 4-col a pure image tile; at 5-col a dense mosaic sliver with only a hovered tooltip. The card self-governs its layout independently using @container, no JS decides card internals. The resize interaction makes container queries the legible mechanic. Distinct from morphing-grid (a fixed set of four named full-grid formations driven by scroll; cards do not self-reshape) and bento (a fixed broken-grid). A 3-col layout is the scroll=0 default with the resize handle visible. No canvas; no WebGL; no rAF. Mobile: column count is fixed responsive CSS; drag handle hidden on touch. Guardrail: @supports (container-type: inline-size) gate; fallback is standard media-query layout; no inline SVG fill/stroke with CSS var() in presentation attributes. Native CSS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: :HAS() CHAIN - CSS :has() relational selectors create cross-card state propagation
ref: CSS :has() selector (Baseline 2024; MDN web-docs relational-pseudo-class 2025-2026; developer.mozilla.org); advanced :has() gallery interactions featured in CSS-Tricks 2026 (css-tricks.com/exploring-css-has/)
palette: #1c1c1e (graphite) + #f0ede8 (bone) + #7b68ee (slate-violet propagation highlight); activated chain is the only color event
brief: A 12-card grid where hovering one card triggers a ripple of state changes in related cards purely via CSS :has() and sibling combinators: the hovered card's parent section has-checks for :hover and dims all non-sibling cards; cards that share a data-tag attribute with the hovered card gain a violet propagation ring without any JavaScript. A second :has() rule makes the grid container itself shift its background when any card with data-featured is hovered. Zero JS handles the visual logic - the entire cross-card relational behavior is declarative CSS. Distinct from magnetic-grid-snap (JS verlet spring physics pulling cards toward cursor; JS decides every transform) and proximity-glyph-field (JS rAF measures cursor distance per glyph). The relational selector chain is the interaction paradigm, not the visual motif. At scroll=0 one card is in :focus-within state showing the propagation ring. No canvas; no WebGL; no JS for visual logic. Mobile: :hover becomes :focus (keyboard/tap navigable). Guardrail: @supports selector(:has(*)) gate; fallback is hover-only opacity. Native CSS/HTML only, NO GSAP or JS for visual state. ONE small JS block only for accessibility focus management.

name: FILTER FORGE - inline SVG feTurbulence filters live-morph all media on scroll
ref: SVG feTurbulence / feDisplacementMap filter animation pattern referenced in Codrops 'Advanced SVG Filter Effects' (2025-2026, tympanus.net/codrops/tag/svg-filter/); generative SVG filter aesthetics in CSS Design Awards digital-art category 2026 (cssdesignawards.com)
palette: #0a0a0a (void) + #f5f2ec (bone image surface); SVG filter output IS the color and texture system, no palette fills
brief: All media (images and short loops) is passed through an inline SVG filter chain: feTurbulence (animated baseFrequency via SMIL) + feDisplacementMap warps each work like melting emulsion on scroll; a feColorMatrix shifts saturation toward a target hue tied to the scroll position. Each work panel has its own named filter with a distinct turbulence octave so no two looks identical. At rest the filters are subtle; accelerating scroll amplifies distortion. Distinct from liquid-distortion (WebGL GLSL displacement shader via Three.js; separate canvas element) and glitch-datamosh (RGB split + scanline block; Canvas 2D compositing with ImageData). This uses only declarative inline SVG filter primitives - no canvas, no WebGL, no JS-driven pixel operations. Signature at scroll=0: first work is already mildly turbulence-warped with visible emulsion-melt. Mobile: feDisplacementMap scale halved for GPU budget. Guardrail: only two concurrent SVG filter animations active (IntersectionObserver pauses off-screen); no feBlend on video (static image fallback); reduced-motion sets baseFrequency to 0 (flat filter). All SVG fill/stroke values are hex literals, not CSS var(). Native CSS/JS/SVG only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: DIAGONAL DRIFT - counter-rotating diagonal marquee bands carry works across the viewport
ref: Diagonal marquee / ticker-band layouts featured in Awwwards experimental-design SOTD 2026 (awwwards.com/inspiration_search/experimental/); Godly 2026 diagonal-scroll category (godly.design/sites/) citing studios such as Bureau Borsche and DIA Studio
brief: The page is a set of 4 full-width diagonal marquee bands tilted at alternating +6deg/-6deg. Each band is an infinite-looping row of work thumbnails that scrolls horizontally; adjacent bands counter-rotate their scroll direction so they drift apart at the seam. Vertical scroll controls a global speed multiplier: slow scroll = drift barely; fast scroll = bands race apart and blur. Clicking any thumbnail snaps it full-screen with a vermilion overlay. Distinct from vertical-ticker (a vertical broadcast stock-tape crawl; single direction; click expands a quote card in situ; monospace amber aesthetic) and never-ending-parallax (three depth layers advancing vertically with Z-parallax; no diagonal tilt bands). The diagonal band composition fills the viewport at scroll=0 already in motion. Pure CSS animation (animation: scroll linear infinite) + JS speed multiplier on --speed custom property. No canvas; no WebGL. Mobile: bands horizontal only (tilt removed; 2 bands). Guardrail: band content duplicated once in DOM for seamless loop (no JS cloning loop); speed multiplier capped at 5x; reduced-motion pauses all bands (thumbnail grid static). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: RISO RUN - real-time two-color risograph print emulation with ink-bleed and misregistration
ref: Risograph print revival cited in Awwwards print-inspired-web SOTD collection 2026 (awwwards.com/inspiration_search/print/); riso digital emulation techniques in CSS Design Awards illustration category 2026 (cssdesignawards.com); print-to-screen aesthetic studios including Hato Press and Set Weekly
palette: #f5f0e5 (newsprint) + fluorescent pink #ff69a0 + teal #009898 (the two riso inks; misregistration is the third visual layer)
brief: Every image and section is rendered as a two-color risograph print in real time: a Canvas 2D pipeline separates each source image into pink and teal halftone dot layers at different angles (pink 45deg, teal 75deg), applies a slight random offset per page-load to simulate misregistration, and paints ink-bleed diffusion at dot edges using a box-blur kernel. Scrolling slightly shifts the misregistration offset so the two ink layers breathe. Distinct from dithered-lo-fi (1-bit Bayer-matrix dither in a WebGL shader; monochrome only; no halftone color separation) and zine-collage (torn-paper clip-path + tape-pin collage aesthetic; duotone CSS filter, not a per-pixel halftone pipeline). The technique is the visual paradigm, not a stylistic overlay. First panel is fully riso-processed at scroll=0. Canvas 2D halftone pipeline (getImageData -> dot-grid -> putImageData per panel); panels processed once on load then cached as dataURL. Mobile: identical; dot size increases for readability at smaller size. Guardrail: source image downsampled to max 400x400 before halftone processing; processing runs once off rAF on load (not per frame); reduced-motion freezes misregistration drift. No WebGL; native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: PLAYABLE - an 80-col text-mode game IS the portfolio, works are unlockable levels
ref: Terminal/playable portfolio category cited in Awwwards experimental-developer SOTD 2026 (awwwards.com/inspiration_search/creative-developer/); text-mode game portfolio aesthetic referenced in Godly 2026 playable-web collection (godly.design/sites/); studios including Play Studio and Monopo Tokyo
palette: #0c0c0c (terminal void) + #00cc66 (phosphor green) + #e8c547 (amber collectible); ANSI color only, no true-color fills
brief: The portfolio is a playable 80-column ANSI text-mode adventure: the visitor navigates a map drawn in box-drawing characters, collects prompt-fragments as items, and unlocking a door reveals a work (full-bleed image with ANSI-frame border). Arrow keys or WASD move a @ player character around rooms; each room description is the prompt that made its work. A HUD strip shows collected fragments and a score counter. The entire game state is a plain JS object (no engine library). Distinct from boot-sequence (animated BIOS init revealing a portfolio; viewer is passive) and stdout (a developer control-room with a CLI aesthetic; viewer reads, does not play) and interface-nostalgia-os (a faux Win95 desktop with draggable windows; not a game with state and movement). The player @ is on the opening room map at scroll=0 with the first door visible. No canvas; no WebGL; DOM spans for the grid, keyboard events for movement. Mobile: on-screen arrow pad replaces keyboard. Guardrail: map drawn from a 2D array (JSON); no procedural generation mid-session; reduced-motion collapses movement to instant teleport; no inline SVG. Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: OPEN STUDIO - simulated collaborative-cursor gallery with named visitors and live trace
ref: Real-time collaborative cursor aesthetic cited in Linear, Figma, and Liveblocks design showcase 2026; collaborative-presence UI pattern in Awwwards design-systems SOTD 2026 (awwwards.com/inspiration_search/ux/); studios including Basement Studio and Vercel
palette: #0f0f12 (void) + per-cursor accent from a seed palette: cobalt #1a3fc4, coral #e8534a, lime #8cc63f, violet #7b3fe4 + bone labels #f0ece4
brief: The gallery simulates a live multi-user collaborative session: 3 to 5 animated fake-visitor cursors drift around the page leaving fading trace trails, each labeled with a name and a colored dot; hovering a work triggers a simulated annotation bubble from one visitor ("Aiko: love the lighting here"). The visitor's own real cursor is one of the named presence dots. Each work card shows a tiny avatar stack of who is "looking" at it. The entire collaboration layer is pure JS simulation (no WebSocket): cursors follow smooth Bezier paths seeded by Math.random with a stable visitor-id seed, annotating on an interval. Distinct from particle-constellation (a star-field node map with constellation lines; no presence or cursor simulation) and infinite-canvas (a spatial pan/zoom mood board with connector wires; no multi-user presence aesthetic). The opening state at scroll=0 shows three cursors already drifting and one annotation bubble visible. Canvas 2D draws cursor trace trails; DOM elements for cursors and labels. Mobile: simulated cursors animate but smaller; no real cursor shown. Guardrail: cursor path recalculated only every 2s (not rAF per cursor); trace canvas at DPR 1; reduced-motion stops cursor drift (static presence dots only). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.

name: PARTICLE SORT - works encoded as particles that self-organize into data-driven formations
ref: Data-physics / particle formation techniques in Codrops 'Particles Forming Images' (2025, tympanus.net/codrops/tag/particles/); generative-data-viz portfolio aesthetic cited in Observable Plot and Godly 2026 data-art collection (godly.design/sites/)
palette: #050508 (void) + #e8f5e9 (ice-green particles rest state) + #ffcf6e (amber sort-target highlight) + #c0392b (vermilion active formation label)
brief: Each work is encoded as a particle cloud (100 to 200 particles per work, positions seeded from the image's pixel luminance grid). The full corpus of works floats as a galaxy of particle clouds in a Canvas 2D viewport. Clicking a sort button (by date, by color-temperature, by generation-time) fires a physics simulation: each particle springs toward its sorted-formation position using a simple attraction force, particles belonging to the same work stay loosely bonded, and the formation settles after ~1.5s. Hovering a cluster reveals the work thumbnail and metadata. Distinct from particle-constellation (a static star-map of work-nodes with constellation lines; nodes do not spring into data-sorted formations) and draggable-scatter (explicit drag-throw physics on card DOM elements, not particle swarms). The full galaxy of unsorted particle clouds is the scroll=0 state. Pure Canvas 2D; no WebGL. Mobile: particle count halved per work; touch triggers sort. Guardrail: total particle count capped at 2,000; spring integrator capped at 120 steps then snaps; rAF pauses when tab is hidden; reduced-motion skips spring animation (instant formation snap). Native CSS/JS only, NO GSAP or external libs.
